title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a citizen science project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Eva, The Bird Counter
Eva joined the bird survey because she liked sitting quietly in the park before work, usually with coffee cooling beside her notebook. [47]She soon learned that counting birds meant recording only what she could identify confidently, not guessing at every distant movement in the trees.[/47] Her habit of keeping detailed travel diaries helped her note weather, time and location consistently. [48]The project coordinator praised her records because they included repeated observations from the same bench over several months.[/48] Eva found it frustrating when joggers or dogs scattered the birds just as she raised her binoculars. Still, she began noticing patterns she had once ignored, such as which shrubs filled first after rain. She says the survey changed a pleasant walk into an act of attention. Now she recognises individual blackbirds by behaviour, although she refuses to give them names in case sentiment affects her notes or tempts her to invent patterns.
texts.B:
### Tomas, The Air Quality Monitor
Tomas volunteered to maintain small air sensors outside shops and schools on streets chosen by the university team. [49]His first mistake was placing one sensor too close to a takeaway vent, which made the readings look far worse than the street actually was.[/49] As a former electrician, he was comfortable fixing brackets and replacing batteries, but the data rules were new to him. [50]He learned to photograph each sensor after maintenance so researchers could check whether anything nearby had changed.[/50] The work made him more aware of delivery traffic, idling cars and the difference a windy day could make. Tomas enjoyed the practical nature of the role, yet he was surprised by the responsibility of producing trustworthy numbers. [55]He now tells new volunteers that a badly placed sensor can be worse than no sensor, because it creates confidence in the wrong answer, especially when campaigners are desperate for clear evidence.[/55]
texts.C:
### Mei, The Pond Life Recorder
Mei helped monitor insects and tiny freshwater animals in ponds on a restored industrial site near the canal. [51]She had to learn how to take samples gently, because stirring the mud too much made the water impossible to examine.[/51] Her training as an art teacher helped when drawing quick pencil sketches of creatures that would not stay still under the lens. [52]The most exciting moment was finding a species that had not been recorded there since the factories closed.[/52] At first, Mei expected scientific work to feel precise, but it also required humility when experts corrected her identifications. She became fond of the slow rhythm: dip, wait, look, compare, release. The project taught her that recovery is often visible only to people who know where and how to look. She now carries spare socks, having learned that pond edges are never as firm as they appear after a week of rain.
texts.D:
### Noah, The Night Sky Logger
Noah joined a light-pollution project after his daughter complained that she had never seen the Milky Way from their small back garden. [53]He measured sky brightness from the same street corner every clear month, even when the novelty had worn off.[/53] His experience as a bus driver helped him choose safe stopping places and understand how street layouts affected glare. [54]The hardest part was waiting twenty minutes for his eyes to adjust before taking a reading, especially in winter.[/54] He felt ridiculous at first standing alone with a clipboard beside parked cars, but neighbours gradually began asking what he had seen. [56]Noah says the project gave darkness a value in his mind, not as emptiness but as something communities can protect together through planning and habit.[/56] He now notices new bright signs and shop window lights immediately, like extra noise in a familiar song, and he writes each change down.

questions.question: learned not to record uncertain sightings as definite data?
questions.explanation: Eva recorded only birds she could identify confidently.

questions.question: was praised for repeated observations from one consistent location?
questions.explanation: Eva's records came from the same bench over several months.

questions.question: placed a measuring device somewhere that distorted the results?
questions.explanation: Tomas put a sensor too close to a takeaway vent.

questions.question: documented equipment after visits so researchers could spot environmental changes?
questions.explanation: Tomas photographed sensors so changes nearby could be checked.

questions.question: had to avoid disturbing material that would make observation difficult?
questions.explanation: Mei learned not to stir mud because it made the water impossible to examine.

questions.question: found something that had been absent from records for many years?
questions.explanation: Mei found a species not recorded there since the factories closed.

questions.question: kept measuring from one spot long after the activity stopped feeling new?
questions.explanation: Noah measured from the same corner every clear month after the novelty passed.

questions.question: found the delay needed before measuring particularly difficult in cold weather?
questions.explanation: Noah had to wait for his eyes to adjust, especially in winter.

questions.question: says unreliable equipment placement can be more harmful than having no data?
questions.explanation: Tomas says a badly placed sensor can be worse than no sensor.

questions.question: says the project made them see absence of artificial brightness as worth protecting?
questions.explanation: Noah says the project gave darkness value as something communities can protect.
